questions.question: Define a distributed system according to Tanenbaum and van Steen and explain the significance of the phrase 'single coherent system'.
keyPoints: Definition: Collection of independent computers
Appears to users as a single system
Significance: Unified view hides distributed nature
Users interact without knowing underlying complexity
Independent computers coordinate via network

questions.question: List and explain any four types of transparency in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Access transparency: Local/remote access indistinguishable
Location transparency: Resource location hidden from users
Migration transparency: Resources can move without user knowledge
Replication transparency: Multiple copies appear as one
Concurrency transparency: Parallel access appears sequential
Failure transparency: Faults are masked from users

questions.question: What are the three main categories of distributed systems? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Distributed Computing Systems: High-performance computing (cluster, grid, cloud)
Distributed Information Systems: Business applications (ERP, banking systems)
Distributed Pervasive Systems: Embedded/ubiquitous (sensor networks, IoT)
Clear distinction based on application domain

questions.question: Explain middleware and its role in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Middleware is a software layer between OS and applications
Provides common services and communication facilities
Handles heterogeneity in hardware/software
Offers transparency to applications
Examples: CORBA, DCOM, Java RMI

questions.question: What is scalability? Discuss the three dimensions of scalability in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Scalability: Ability to handle growth by adding resources
Size scalability: Adding more users/resources
Geographic scalability: Users distributed across wider areas
Administrative scalability: Managing more organizations/domains
Challenges in achieving scalability: communication overhead, consistency

questions.question: Differentiate between a distributed system and a computer network.
keyPoints: Computer network: Infrastructure for communication
Distributed system: Software built on network infrastructure
Key difference: Middleware provides unified view in DS
Networks just transport data; DS provides services
DS requires coordination; network just enables connection

questions.question: Explain the client-server model in distributed systems with a suitable diagram.
keyPoints: Client: Initiates requests for services
Server: Provides services on request
Client-server separation enables modularity
Server waits passively for client requests
Examples: Web browsers (client) and web servers

questions.question: Discuss the main goals of distributed systems and explain how each contributes to making distributed systems effective.
keyPoints: Transparency: Hides distribution complexity from users
Openness: Uses standard protocols, allows interoperability
Scalability: Handles growth by adding resources
Reliability/Fault Tolerance: Continues operation despite failures
Performance: Efficient resource utilization
Resource Sharing: Shares expensive resources
Each goal addresses specific challenges in distributed environments

questions.question: What are the key challenges in designing distributed systems? Discuss heterogeneity, openness, security, scalability, and failure handling.
keyPoints: Heterogeneity: Different hardware, OS, languages must interoperate
Openness: Standard interfaces needed for extensibility
Security: Distributed nature increases attack surfaces
Scalability: Hard to add resources without performance loss
Failure Handling: Partial failures common, must handle gracefully
Concurrency: Multiple users accessing shared resources
Transparency vs performance trade-offs

questions.question: Explain the advantages of distributed systems over centralized systems. Also discuss any two disadvantages.
keyPoints: Advantages:
- Reliability/Fault tolerance through redundancy
- Scalability - incremental growth possible
- Resource sharing - expensive peripherals shared
- Cost effectiveness - parallel processing
- Flexibility - workload distribution possible
- Openness - heterogeneous components
Disadvantages:
- Complexity - harder to develop and maintain
- Security - more vulnerable points
- Network dependency - communication issues
- Data inconsistency risks
